title: Fettuccine ai funghi porcini
subtitle: Fettuccine with porcini in a cream sauce
excerpt: Dried porcini soaked and sautéed with their soaking liquid, shallot, and garlic, finished with cream and folded through fettuccine. A sauce that tastes profoundly of the forest and takes 25 minutes to make.
sourceNotes: Based on the Italian pasta ai funghi porcini tradition documented in Marcella Hazan, 'Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking' (1992) and Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006). Dried porcini are used here rather than fresh, making the recipe available year-round.

content.text: Dried porcini, reconstituted in warm water, have an intensity of flavour that fresh porcini approach only when very fresh and in peak season. The soaking liquid is as important as the mushrooms themselves: strained carefully to remove grit, it goes into the sauce and reduces to a dark, deeply savoury base. Nothing about this recipe should be rushed — the mushrooms and shallot need time to concentrate, and the cream needs time to reduce into the mushroom liquid.

Tagliatelle or fettuccine are the right pasta for this sauce: wide ribbons that hold the creamy sauce in their folds and carry pieces of mushroom in every bite. Parmesan is added at the table, not stirred through — it would make the sauce too thick.

items.prepNote: soaked in 250 ml warm water for 20 minutes

content.text: Soak {{dried-porcini}} of dried porcini in 250 ml of warm water for 20 minutes. Lift out the mushrooms with your hands, squeezing gently. Roughly chop the large pieces. Strain the soaking liquid through a fine sieve into a jug, discarding the last tablespoon (which holds the grit).

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{shallots}} diced shallots and cook for 5 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} garlic and {{thyme}} thyme leaves. Cook for 1 minute. Add the mushrooms and cook for 3 minutes until any remaining moisture evaporates. Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and let it bubble until almost gone. Pour in the strained porcini liquid and simmer for 5 minutes until reduced by half. Add {{double-cream}} of cream and simmer for 3 minutes until lightly thickened. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper-ground}} of pepper.

Cook {{fettuccine}} of fettuccine in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a cup of pasta water, drain, and toss through the sauce with a splash of pasta water to loosen. Divide between bowls. Scatter {{parsley}} chopped parsley and serve with {{parmesan}} parmesan.

items.cause: Porcini soaking liquid not strained carefully
items.fix: Strain through a fine sieve or coffee filter. The grit sinks to the bottom of the soaking liquid — leave the last tablespoon behind.

items.cause: Porcini soaking liquid not reduced enough
items.fix: Reduce the soaking liquid more aggressively before adding the cream. The liquid holds most of the flavour.

content.text: Porcini mushrooms are gathered across northern and central Italy from late summer through autumn, and the pasta ai funghi porcini is one of the seasonal dishes most anticipated by Italian cooks. Fresh porcini have a short season; dried porcini preserve their flavour through the year and are available in most supermarkets. The Emilian version uses fettuccine or tagliatelle; Tuscan versions are often simpler, with just the mushroom-soaking liquid and olive oil rather than cream. Both are good; the cream version is milder and more forgiving.
